judgments.title: BELLWORTH DEVELOPMENTS SDN BHD v SETIAKON BUILDERS SDN BHD
judgments.catchwords: The Court of Appeal reaffirmed the principle of minimal judicial intervention in arbitration. A procedural breach, such as an alleged one-day delay in submitting a draft award for AIAC technical review, does not automatically invalidate an arbitral award where the requirement is merely directory, no timely objection was raised, and no prejudice is shown. An arbitral award will not be set aside on the basis of a purported “new difference” where the issue was within the parties’ pleadings, evidence and submissions. Courts will not review the merits of an award under the guise of a setting-aside application.

judgments.catchwords: 1. This dispute concerns the legal characterisation of certain areas within a mixed strata development undertaken by the Third Defendant, namely whether they constitute common property vested in the Plaintiff Management Corporation or whether they were lawfully designated and dealt with as accessory parcels. The Plaintiff, representing the parcel proprietors, contends that the areas in question form part of the common property and were wrongly alienated. The First, Second and Third Defendants dispute this contention and maintain that the relevant areas were validly created and transferred in accordance with the applicable statutory regime governing strata developments. 4. The primary issue for determination is whether the areas represented by accessory parcels A1144, A1154, A1155, A1156 and A1157 were lawfully created as accessory parcels pursuant to the strata subdivision process, or whether they remained, in law, part of the common property which vested in the Plaintiff upon the establishment of the Management Corporation. 5. The resolution of that issue necessarily requires this Court to consider the statutory framework governing strata developments, the legal effect of the approved and certified strata plans, the circumstances surrounding the subsequent transfers of the impugned parcels, and the application of the doctrine of indefeasibility of title.
judgments.judge: YA Dr Choong Yeow Choy

names: AHMAD SADLI @ AHMAD FADLI BIN MAJID

judgments.catchwords: CIVIL PROCEDURE Parties — Locus standi — Shifting capacity — Suit commenced by court-appointed committee under the Mental Health Act 2001 — Demise of patient during pendency of suit — Automatic dissolution of committee by operation of law — Standing of heir to maintain action in personal capacity as beneficiary of the estate — Rules of Court 2012, Order 15 CONTRACT Capacity — Unsoundness of mind — Mental capacity to execute contract — Sale and Purchase Agreement — Contracts Act 1950, Sections 11 & 12 — Allegation that vendor was suffering from advanced cognitive impairment and Alzheimer's disease at material times — Presumption of cognitive capacity — Lucid intervals — Burden of proving incapacity on the party asserting unsoundness of mind — Evaluation of contemporaneous conduct and execution of complex signature Consideration — Validity of payment — Payment of purchase price to vendor’s designated stakeholder — Stakeholder's receipt — Standard conveyancing practice — Clear bank records showing immediate debit of funds on date of execution — Purchaser’s complete discharge of contractual obligation — Subsequent non-disbursement of funds by stakeholder to vendor — Whether failure of consideration — Contracts Act 1950, Section 2(d) LAND LAW Indefeasibility of title and interest — Registration of transfer — National Land Code (1965) (or Act 828), Section 340 — Challenging registered title on grounds of void instrument, fraud, and misrepresentation — Shield of immediate indefeasibility — Failure of challenger to establish statutory exceptions under Section 340(2) Land Inquiry — Section 27 National Land Code — Delay in registration of transfer — Statutory land inquiry triggered by private caveat — In-person appearance of vendor before Land Administrator — Finding of mental capacity and voluntariness by a public authority — Statutory legitimacy of registration EVIDENCE Expert evidence — Admissibility and weight — Clash of medical experts — Attending neurologist vs. independent consultant psychiatrist — Rules of Court 2012, Order 40A — Fatal non-compliance with statutory declaration requirements by attending physician — Evidentiary value of retrospective document-based psychiatric clinical audit — Admissibility of medical records and MRI findings — Clinical-ethical principle of patient autonomy in geriatric psychiatry Presumptions — Presumption of regularity — Evidence Act 1950, Section 114(e) — Execution of Memorandum of Transfer (Borang 14A) witnessed by Assistant Land Registrar — Whether official acts presumed to be regularly and honestly performed in the absence of cogent evidence to the contrary Adverse inference — Section 114(g), Evidence Act 1950 — Failure of Defendant to call transaction broker and vendor's personal employee — Sufficiency of primary documentary evidence to establish facts — No gaps in evidence — Burden of proof on Plaintiff to establish assertions EQUITY Equitable defences — Laches, acquiescence, and inordinate delay — Failure of heirs to challenge land transaction during the vendor's lifetime — Eight-year delay in commencing legal action — Serious prejudice to the purchaser due to loss of the vendor's direct testimony — Doctrine of clean hands

judgments.title: SIVACHANDRAN A/L S JAYARAJAH v Pendakwa Raya
judgments.catchwords: Review jurisdiction - Rule 137 of the Rules of the Federal Court 1995 -Inherent powers of Federal Court – All sentences passed to be in accordance with the law – Avoiding miscarriage of justice - Exceptional circumstances - Fresh evidence of prisoner’s death following whipping. Constitutional Law - Federal Constitution - Articles 5(1), 8(1) and 8(2) Fundamental liberties - Life and personal liberty - Human dignity - Equal protection of the law - Whether it is in violation of medical ethics for medical officer to be participate in the execution of whipping. Judicial whipping - Corporal punishment - Whether judicial whipping amounts to cruel, inhuman and degrading treatment (“CIDT”) - Whether punishment of whipping is inconsistent with constitutional protection of life, dignity and bodily integrity. Whether whipping is disproportionate and therefore contrary to Article 8(1) - Whether exemption of women from whipping constitutes prohibited discrimination on ground only of gender is contrary to Art 8(2) - Intelligible differentia. Whether whipping is against Art. 5 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (“UDHR”) and the ASEAN Human Rights Declaration in its prohibitions against CIDT - Whether whipping is against customary international law.

judgments.title: NOMALAN A/L RAMAYAH v Pendakwa Raya
judgments.catchwords: Abolition of Mandatory Death Penalty Act 2023 – Child sentence under Section 97(2) of the Child Act 2001- "detained in prison during the pleasure of the Yang di-Pertuan Agong (King) or Ruler"- does not violate Article 5 because the child offender is convicted after the due process of a trial and at its conclusion, the sentence passed is in accordance with a validly passed law- Doctrine of reasonable classification- doctrine of proportionality-ultimate objective was to place child offenders in a category of their own so as to not suffer the extreme fate of adult offenders with the view to being given an opportunity at rehabilitation and their eventual re-integration back into society- reasonable nexus that existed between the discrimination and that legislative aim sought to be achieved-no violation or fundamental breach of Article 8-alternative remedies provided for under the law for the Applicants to be dealt with according to Section 97(4) of the Child Act 2001, Regulation 54 and 113 of the Prison Regulations 2000 read together with power of pardon under Article 42 of the Federal Constitution-remedy afforded exclusively to the Applicants via section 97(5) of the Child Act 2001 provides the opportunity for them to be released earlier from detention, by virtue of the yearly recommendation by the Board of Visiting Justices. This opportunity is not afforded to a convict of the age of majority- Section 2 and Section 3 of Act 847 makes in clear that the pre-condition for the application under Section 3 is that the person must be on death row at the date of enforcement of Act 847- Applicants’ squarely outside the purview of Act 847
judgments.judge: YA Dato' Collin Lawrence Sequerah
coram: YAA Tun Wan Ahmad Farid Bin Wan Salleh
YAA Datuk Hajah Azizah binti Haji Nawawi

judgments.catchwords: Abolition of Mandatory Death Penalty Act 2023 – Child sentence under Section 97(2) of the Child Act 2001- "detained in prison during the pleasure of the Yang di-Pertuan Agong (King) or Ruler"- does not violate Article 5 because the child offender is convicted after the due process of a trial and at its conclusion, the sentence passed is in accordance with a validly passed law- Doctrine of reasonable classification- doctrine of proportionality-ultimate objective was to place child offenders in a category of their own so as to not suffer the extreme fate of adult offenders with the view to being given an opportunity at rehabilitation and their eventual re-integration back into society- reasonable nexus that existed between the discrimination and that legislative aim sought to be achieved-no violation or fundamental breach of Article 8-alternative remedies provided for under the law for the Applicants to be dealt with according to Section 97(4) of the Child Act 2001, Regulation 54 and 113 of the Prison Regulations 2000 read together with power of pardon under Article 42 of the Federal Constitution-remedy afforded exclusively to the Applicants via section 97(5) of the Child Act 2001 provides the opportunity for them to be released earlier from detention, by virtue of the yearly recommendation by the Board of Visiting Justices. This opportunity is not afforded to a convict of the age of majority- Section 2 and Section 3 of Act 847 makes in clear that the pre-condition for the application under Section 3 is that the person must be on death row at the date of enforcement of Act 847- Applicants’ squarely outside the purview of Act 847

judgments.title: LIM SIM EE v DSI NORTHERN SOURCE SDN BHD & ors
judgments.catchwords: The Plaintiff commenced this action under section 346 of the Companies Act 2016, alleging that the Defendants conducted the affairs of the 1st Defendant company in an oppressive manner that unfairly prejudiced him as a shareholder. He sought various remedies, including declarations invalidating corporate acts, restoration of his shareholding, a compulsory buy-out of his shares, damages, and consequential relief. The Plaintiff contended that he was a 20% shareholder whose interests were undermined through a series of corporate actions, including the dilution of his shareholding, an alleged breach of a Share Sale Agreement worth RM3 million, transfers and restructuring involving related companies, his removal as a director, and denial of access to company records. He argued that these actions formed a systematic attempt to squeeze him out of the company. After considering the oral and documentary evidence, the Court found that the Plaintiff failed to establish his claim. It held that he had not proven a beneficial interest in the shares, as he admitted he had not paid for them and produced no evidence of consideration. The Court further found that the share dilution resulted from a capital exercise in which the Plaintiff chose not to participate, and that there was insufficient evidence to support the alleged Share Sale Agreement or claims of bad faith, personal loss, or oppressive conduct. The Court concluded that the dispute reflected a breakdown in commercial relations rather than oppression under section 346. Accordingly, the Plaintiff's claim was dismissed, with costs of RM20,000 awarded to the Defendants.
judgments.judge: YA Dato' Anita binti Harun

judgments.title: GAN HENG WAN v YONG KENG FONG & ors
judgments.catchwords: The Plaintiff commenced an Originating Summons under section 346 of the Companies Act 2016, alleging that the 1st and 2nd Defendants had conducted the affairs of the 3rd Defendant oppressively, unfairly prejudicially, or in disregard of his interests as a shareholder. The Plaintiff sought declarations of oppression, an order requiring the Defendants to purchase his shares at fair value, or alternatively, the winding up of the company, together with ancillary injunctions, damages and costs. The Court heard the application alongside the Plaintiff’s application to convert the proceedings into a writ action, which was also dismissed. After considering the affidavit evidence, documentary exhibits, submissions and authorities, the Court found that the Plaintiff failed to establish the requirements for relief under section 346. The evidence demonstrated only commercial disagreements and a breakdown in the parties’ business relationship, rather than oppression. Accordingly, the Originating Summons was dismissed, all reliefs were refused, and costs of RM5,000 were awarded to the Defendants.
judgments.judge: YA Dato' Anita binti Harun

judgments.title: GAN HENG WAN v YONG KENG FONG & ors
judgments.catchwords: The Plaintiff applied by Notice of Application (Enclosure 24) to convert the proceedings from an Originating Summons to a writ action under the Rules of Court 2012, seeking consequential directions, a stay of the Originating Summons hearing, and costs. The Court considered whether the factual disputes were unsuitable for determination on affidavit evidence. After reviewing the affidavits, documentary exhibits, submissions and authorities, the Court held that the Plaintiff failed to demonstrate disputes requiring a full trial. The issues primarily concerned the interpretation and legal effect of WhatsApp messages, financial records and company documents, which could be fairly resolved on affidavit evidence. The Court also found that the Plaintiff’s requests for discovery and witness testimony were insufficiently justified, the challenge to his removal as director could be determined on the existing evidence, and the application was filed late after submissions had been exchanged. Accordingly, the threshold for conversion was not met, Enclosure 24 was dismissed, and costs of RM5,000 were awarded to the Defendants.
judgments.judge: YA Dato' Anita binti Harun

judgments.title: MOHD ZAIRUL BIN MOHD NOOR v Universiti Putra Malaysia (UPM) & ors

parties.label: RESPONDEN
names: Jawatankuasa Pemilih 2/2024 Fakulti Rekabentuk Dan Senibina UPM
Dekan Fakulti Rekabentuk Dan Senibina UPM

judgments.catchwords: The Plaintiff applied for pre-action discovery under Order 24 rule 7A of the Rules of Court 2012, seeking documents relating to the deliberations of the Jawatankuasa Pemilih 2024 Fakulti Rekabentuk dan Senibina Universiti Putra Malaysia concerning his promotion application. He argued that the documents were necessary to determine whether the committee’s decision-making process involved irregularity, bias, or improper considerations that could support a legal challenge. The Defendants opposed the application, arguing that the Plaintiff failed to satisfy the requirements for pre-action discovery and was merely seeking internal committee materials in the hope of finding grounds for a future claim. The Court held that the Plaintiff had not shown the documents were necessary and that his application amounted to an impermissible fishing expedition. Since he already knew the outcome of the promotion exercise and had access to part of the records, the Court dismissed the application. Costs were awarded to the Defendants, without prejudice to the Plaintiff’s right to pursue substantive proceedings if grounds existed.
judgments.judge: YA Dato' Anita binti Harun

judgments.title: RISHIPAL SINGH DHANOA A/L CHARANJIT SINGH (BERNIAGA ATAS NAMA DHANOA ENTERPRISE) v KAWALAN PERTIWI SDN BHD

parties.label: PLAINTIF
names: RISHIPAL SINGH DHANOA A/L CHARANJIT SINGH (BERNIAGA ATAS NAMA DHANOA ENTERPRISE)

judgments.catchwords: These Grounds of Judgment set out the Court’s reasons for dismissing the Plaintiff’s application under Order 18 rule 19 of the Rules of Court 2012 (Enclosure 58) to strike out substantial portions of the Defendant’s Defence. The Plaintiff contended that the Court of Appeal had conclusively determined liability in its favour when allowing the Defendant’s appeal in an earlier setting aside application, thereby giving rise to issue estoppel and rendering the impugned parts of the Defence unsustainable and an abuse of process. The Defendant argued that the Court of Appeal’s decision arose only in the context of interlocutory proceedings and did not amount to a final determination of liability, maintaining that the Defence raised genuine triable issues requiring a full trial. The Court considered, but found it unnecessary to determine, the Defendant’s procedural objection to the Plaintiff’s reliance on multiple limbs of Order 18 rule 19. Even assuming the application was procedurally proper, the Court held that it failed on its merits. The Court found that the Court of Appeal’s earlier decision did not create issue estoppel or res judicata and that the Defence raised substantial issues concerning privity, Stallion Empire, invoices, payment structures and computations. As these matters required full evidential examination, the Defence was not plainly unsustainable. Accordingly, Enclosure 58 was dismissed with costs of RM3,000 to the Defendant, and the matter was directed to proceed to case management.
judgments.judge: YA Dato' Anita binti Harun
